Table 1: Image-level AUROC ↑ and corresponding FM ↓ on MVTec AD dataset [1] after training on the last subdataset. Note that * signifies the usage of a cache pool for rehearsal during training, which may not be possible in real applications. The best results are highlighted in bold, and suboptimal results are highlighted in underline.
Table 2: Pixel-level AUPR ↑ and corresponding FM ↓ on MVTec AD dataset [1] after training on the last subdataset.

Figure 1: Different frameworks in anomaly detection. (A) illustrates the prevalent One-for-One Anomaly Detection paradigm [28, 29, 31]. (B) depicts the unified multiclass model, streamlining the anomaly detection process across multiple classes [42]. (C) showcases the integration of continual learning into anomaly detection [27]. (D) represents our proposed multimodal continual anomaly detection model.
Figure 3: Visualization and analysis of the model performance on the MVTec-AD and VisA Datasets.
Figure 4: Visualization of anomaly segmentation under different modules on the MVTec AD dataset.

Figure 2: The architecture of our model consists of two core components. The Continuous Multimodal Prompts Memory Block (CMPMB) and the Defect Semantics-Guided Adaptive Fusion Mechanism (DSG-AFM). The CMPMB endows the model with continuous learning ability and facilitates unsupervised anomaly detection in continuous scenarios. The DSG-AFM further enhances anomaly detection accuracy by refining the anomaly map based on defect semantics and a dynamic normalization mechanism.
